title: Britain's other age of instability
gist: Britain's current churn of seven prime ministers in ten years is not unprecedented: George III's reign saw the same cycling (and five PMs in under two years after 1782), ended only when the young Pitt combined conviction with pragmatic responsibility to forge a durable governing coalition.

insights.claim: Britain cycling through seven prime ministers in ten years is not unique or unprecedented; the 18th century under George III was at times even more politically unstable than the present.
insights.speaker: Max Skjönsberg
insights.so_what: A historical base rate for UK political churn that argues against treating current instability as terminal decline.
insights.quote: such turbulence is not unique or unprecedented in British political history
insights.locator: As Britain is about to have its seventh prime minister

insights.claim: After the fall of Lord North's government in March 1782, Britain had five prime ministers in less than two years, a churn far exceeding the current rate.
insights.speaker: Max Skjönsberg
insights.so_what: Concrete precedent showing acute instability can resolve into decades of stability.
insights.quote: Britain had five prime ministers in less than two years
insights.locator: the fall of North's government in March 1782

insights.claim: Political instability under George III stemmed less from war than from the king's vision of appointing ministers by loyalty rather than party support, which clashed with a system where partisanship had become structural.
insights.speaker: Max Skjönsberg
insights.so_what: Shows leadership instability can be driven by a structural mismatch between an executive's governing model and the institutional reality.
insights.quote: the instability had its roots in George III's political vision

insights.claim: Appointing a court favourite (the Earl of Bute) with no parliamentary standing produced a short-lived, despised premiership, illustrating that legitimacy among the political class matters more than royal favour.
insights.speaker: Max Skjönsberg
insights.so_what: Leadership lacking institutional buy-in fails quickly regardless of top-down backing.
insights.quote: Bute was a court creature with no parliamentary standing

insights.claim: Lord North achieved 12 years of relative stability not through partisanship but through competence as a parliamentary operator able to conciliate independent backbenchers and competing interests.
insights.speaker: Max Skjönsberg
insights.so_what: Durable leadership in fragmented systems comes from coalition-building skill, not ideological purity.
insights.locator: The king finally found a trusted premier in Lord North

insights.claim: William Pitt the Younger took power at age 24 with only three years as an MP and, against expectation, restored political stability that lasted until the 1820s.
insights.speaker: Max Skjönsberg
insights.so_what: An inexperienced outsider can become a stabilising force given the right blend of skill and pragmatism.
insights.quote: Pitt was only 24 years old and had only served as an MP for three years

insights.claim: Pitt abandoned parliamentary reform after three failed attempts and pivoted to fiscal and administrative reform, demonstrating that politics is the art of the possible.
insights.speaker: Max Skjönsberg
insights.so_what: Effective leaders abandon unwinnable battles and redeploy toward achievable wins.
insights.quote: he quickly learned that politics is the art of the possible

insights.claim: Per Weber's 'Politics as a Vocation', a successful politician must balance the ethic of conviction with the ethic of responsibility, the combination Pitt embodied and the model for any future UK prime minister.
insights.speaker: Max Skjönsberg
insights.so_what: A diagnostic for evaluating leaders: ideological commitment alone or pure pragmatism alone both fail.
insights.quote: combine and balance the ethic of conviction with the ethic of responsibility
